original_abstract: A practical-theological reflection on coaching and equipping children for service as a way to emulating the attitude of Christ. The hypothesis for this research is that the youth is an inherent part of the church. The church, which includes the children, received spiritual gifts from God. The edification of the church is the main purpose in the utilisation of all the gifts. The church received a significant responsibility in equipping and convoying children to be obedient in their calling to be followers of Jesus Christ. Parents and children must use their gifts for their own diakonia. The word diakonia gives expression to the fact that diakonia must take place in accordance to the calling by God. In recent research the expression diakonia also received a more comprehensive meaning. The research field will be explored in four phases and, in doing so, try to come to grips with the current practical-theological situation. In phase one the authors tried to offer a descriptive reflection on research in the practical theology. In phase two the authors undertook exploratory research from the field of the social psychology in order to indicate the prominent role of wrong attitudes and insufficient convoying of children in the praxis. Therefore the authors undertook an intra-disciplinary conversation with the development psychology. In phase three the authors offered basic-theoretical perspectives from Scripture in order to show how the praxis must be. In the last phase of this research practical-theoretical perspectives are explored in order to underline the responsibility of churches in this regard of equipping children in fulfilling their own diakonia. The authors drew a conclusion from their research that children who serve in diakonia and also in obedience to Christ, are also people that are enabled to show the attitude of Jesus Christ towards the world. Parents and other believers must serve in mentoring children. By mentoring children in their diakonia, they enable children and also contribute towards spiritual maturity in their lives.
humanized_abstract: This study investigates coaching and equipping children for service as a paradigm for following the example of Christ. The hypothesis for this study is that the youth is part of the church. The church with children as part of it, received gifts from God and these gifts are utilised for the building up of the church. In terms of the building up of the church, children together with parents and parents bear a huge responsibility of coaching and convoying children to be obedient to their calling as followers of Jesus Christ. In terms of the diakonia of children, the word diakonia is utilised in order to indicate that parents and children together with the church must utilise their gifts for their own diakonia. In recent research diakonia has been given a broader context. This study explores the research field in four phases and therefore attempts to get to the bottom of the practical-theological situation. Phase one attempted a descriptive reflection of research in practical theology. Phase two included an exploratory research from the field of social psychology that showed the incorrect attitudes and lack of convoying of children in the praxis of the church and thus had an intra-disciplinary conversation with the child development psychology. Phase three brought basic-theoretical insights from Scripture in order to show how the praxis of the church should be, and phase four presented practical-theoretical insights in order to confirm the responsibility of the church for coaching and equipping children in their diakonia. The conclusion drawn from the research is that children who are involved in diakonia and therefore who are obedient to Christ, can also be people who show the Jesus Christ attitude towards the world. Therefore parents and other believers must coach children in their diakonia, in order to also bring about spiritual maturity in the lives of the children.
